data.title: Uber: Queen Of Ridesharing And Delivery, But Its Growth Doesn't Stop There
data.description:
Introduction

In large cities, more and more people are choosing not to buy a car. This trend is leading to greater use of alternative means of transport such as trains, subways, and trams. Alongside these, we must not forget ridesharing services, which have been around for some time and are now undergoing a radical transformation. This is where Uber (NYSE:UBER) comes in, the company that has revolutionized the concept of shared mobility and made it a global phenomenon.

At the same time, in recent years, the delivery sector has also experienced significant growth, with more and more people, not only in large cities but also in smaller towns, beginning to use and appreciate these services. Uber, with Uber Eats, has been able to tap into this new consumer habit, further consolidating its presence in the market.

In this phase of evolution in people's habits and needs, Uber is confirming its position as a world leader in mobility and delivery. In this article, I will analyze the company by looking at its most recent financial data, the main risks it faces, and its future growth opportunities.

In a constantly changing world, especially in terms of technology, Uber occupies a unique position. On the one hand, it is undoubtedly a tech company, but on the other, it meets essential, everyday needs, such as transportation and access to food.

According to my analysis, Uber is a company to watch closely in the coming years, as its growth and expansion show no signs of slowing down.

Company Overview

Uber is one of the companies symbolizing the new digital mobility. Through its app, which was initially created as a car sharing service, it now acts as a real intermediary between users who need certain services and others who offer them. The company operates mainly in three segments:

• Mobility: This is the company's core business and allows car owners to register on the platform and offer rides to users, thus turning their cars into a source of income.

• Delivery: With Uber Eats, it connects restaurants, bars, and other businesses with customers who want food or drinks delivered to their door, thanks to a network of drivers.

• Freight: Currently the least profitable segment, Uber has developed a digital marketplace that connects companies that need freight transport with available truckers, optimizing loads and reducing downtime.

Financial Situation

From a financial standpoint, after years of losses caused by revenues that were initially too low compared to high operating costs, Uber reached a milestone in 2023 by achieving a positive net income.

Today, Mobility remains the most profitable segment, but Delivery is growing the fastest. By 2034, Food Delivery Market is expected to reach $637 billion with a CAGR of 10.59%, while the Mobility market is expected to reach $619.72 billion with a CAGR of 12.9%.

In its latest Annual Report, Uber reported revenues of $43.978 million (+18% compared to 2023), a sharp increase compared to previous years and driven mainly by Mobility and Delivery.

User metrics are also positive. In 2024, MAPCs, or monthly active users, increased by 14% compared to 2023, while trips taken grew by 19%, with an average of 66 trips per user per year compared to 63 in the previous year.

Growth continued in 2025. In the second quarter, active users rose from 171 million at the end of 2024 to 180 million, while trips also grew from 3,068 million to 3,268 million in Q2.

The company often refers in its reports to Gross Bookings, i.e., gross revenues that also include taxes and commissions paid to drivers, which reached $46.756 billion in Q2, a total increase of 17%.

• Mobility: +16%

• Delivery: +20%

• Freight: –1%

More importantly, net revenues showed higher growth rates with a total increase of 18% compared to Q2 2024:

• Mobility: +19%

• Delivery: +25%

• Freight: -1%

Looking ahead, Uber expects gross bookings to grow between 17% and 21% in the third quarter, reaching between $48.25 billion and $49.75 billion.

Considering a margin between net revenues and gross bookings of 27%, estimated net revenues would be around $13 billion in Q3. On an annual basis, the company could therefore exceed $50 billion in revenue in 2025, with a conservative scenario of $50.6 billion and a more optimistic one that, with an average growth of 15% per quarter, could push the total to around $52 billion. This is in line with analysts' estimates.

Uber is now present in over 70 countries worldwide, with a strong concentration in the United States and Canada, Latin America, Europe (excluding Russia), the Middle East, Africa, and parts of Asia-Pacific (excluding China and Southeast Asia). The most significant markets remain America, where revenues grew by 15.6%, and Europe, with an even more marked increase of 26.5%.

Competitor Analysis

On the competitor side, Uber faces fierce rivals. In the mobility sector, its main competitors are Lyft (LYFT), which operates mainly in the United States with revenues of $5.365 billion in 2024, and Grab Holdings Limited (GRAB), which operates in Southeast Asia with a model similar to Uber and revenues of approximately $1 billion in the mobility segment. In this scenario, Uber, with its $25 billion in revenues in 2024, represents approximately 80% of the global market share (excluding China's DiDi, which currently operates mainly in China).

In delivery, on the other hand, its main rivals are Door Dash (DASH) in the United States and Delivery Hero (DHERO) in Europe. In China, the giant is Meituan (OTCPK:MPNGF), which, however, does not yet operate internationally, despite having declared its intentions for future expansion. Today, Uber Eats is the world leader in revenue generated in food delivery with a 22.20% market share compared to its main competitors.

Risks and Challenges

Uber's competitive advantage lies primarily in its network: more drivers and more users mean an increasingly efficient and convenient service. Tools such as Uber One, the multi-platform subscription that offers exclusive discounts and benefits with more than 36 million registered users, drive customer loyalty.

Thanks to this vast amount of data, the company is seeking to make the most of it with Uber Journey Ads, an advertising platform that allows brands to launch targeted and personalized campaigns, increasing the prospects for profitability.

On the innovation front, Uber has entered into strategic partnerships, such as the one with Aurora for the development of self-driving vehicles.

According to Uber's estimates, there are future growth opportunities in this segment worth $1 trillion in the US alone.

The transition to autonomous driving is one of the great challenges of the coming years: it is already possible to rent a driverless car in some cities, and companies such as Waymo, Tesla, Zoox, and Motional are accelerating this technology, with the possibility that they will become direct competitors.

The risk is clear, as the implementation of autonomous driving involves numerous obstacles, such as the need to ensure maximum safety, the need for a unified regulatory framework, and the need to build a widespread network that allows these vehicles to circulate.

At the same time, Uber's strength lies in its nature as a platform that connects supply and demand. Users tend to prefer a single app that integrates multiple services rather than downloading separate applications. For this reason, in the future, Uber may not only compete with autonomous driving players but also collaborate with them.

One of the most significant risks concerns the classification of drivers. Today, Uber considers them independent contractors, free to decide when to work and whether to use competing platforms. However, legal disputes and legislative discussions are underway in several countries to redefine their status. If they were considered employees, the company would have to bear much higher costs in wages, contributions, and benefits, with a negative impact on margins and cash flows.

Another risk is related to the very nature of the delivery business, which has low barriers to entry but high operating costs. To be profitable, Uber must maintain a delicate balance between three factors: a large driver base, a sufficient number of commercial partners, and a large user base willing to use the service. If even one of these elements is missing, profitability suffers and users are likely to migrate to competitors.

Valuation

At the time of the valuation, Uber has a market price of $92.81.

I carried out the valuation based on three scenarios: Base, Bearish, and Best. In all three scenarios, I calculated:

• WACC: 11.24% (year 2026) until settling at 9.16% (year 2035), when I expect the company to stabilize.

• Long-term growth rate: 3%

• EBIT Margin: growing to 30% in 2035.

Base case

In the Base case, I assumed an average revenue growth rate of 12% over 10 years, in line with analysts' estimates and with the growth of the Food Delivery Market and the Mobility Market.

Bearish case

In the bearish case, I assumed an average revenue growth rate of 10% over 10 years. I predicted this growth because I expect that, in the worst-case scenario, the company can grow without losing market share.

Best case

In the best case, I predicted an average revenue growth of 14.27% over 10 years.

Conclusion

In summary, Uber is currently experiencing strong growth, with revenues increasing by an average of 23% per year between 2018 and 2024. It is a global leader in the mobility sector and is also establishing a solid foothold in food delivery. The main challenge for the company will be to consolidate its profitability while maintaining its leadership in key markets and addressing the technological and regulatory changes that could transform the sector in the coming years.

I believe that Uber offers excellent growth opportunities and can drive the expansion of the delivery and mobility sectors. The advent of autonomous driving will certainly open up new scenarios, but in my opinion, it will be a long time before it is adopted on a large scale globally. Today, Uber is already very strong in America, is growing in Europe, and, thanks to targeted investments (as it is already doing), could expand into high-potential markets such as India and Asia in general.

In light of these scenarios, my assessment of the company is decidedly positive.
